notes.note: Cicero peppers the letter with Greek medical terms for Tiro's regimen: diaphoresis (sweating-out/dispersal), pepsis (digestion), akopia (a relaxing rub-down to relieve fatigue), a measured walk (peripatos symmetros), tripsis (massage), and easy bowels (eulysia koilias).

notes.anchor: Stir up Parhedrus to take the kitchen-garden on lease himself
notes.note: Parhedrus is a man (apparently a freedman or dependent) whom Cicero wants to bid for the lease of the Tusculan kitchen-garden, so as to shame the regular gardener (holitor) into offering more.

notes.anchor: That good-for-nothing Helico used to pay a thousand sesterces for it
notes.note: Helico was a former lessee of the garden who paid HS 1000 even when the plot had no amenities; Cicero is haggling to extract a better rent now that it has been improved.
The Crabra was a stream near Tusculum whose water was apportioned by allotment among the local estates; rights to it were a recurring concern of the Tusculan landowners.

notes.anchor: composing some piece in the Sophoclean style
notes.note: A teasing jab at Tiro's literary ambitions: Cicero asks whether his secretary, with leisure on his hands, is writing tragedy in the grand manner of the Athenian tragedian Sophocles.

notes.anchor: A. Ligurius, a friend of Caesar's, has died
notes.note: Aulus Ligurius, a mutual friend close to Caesar; his death is reported as ordinary news between intimates.
